title: Missouri River

infobox.value: The [Missouri tribe](/wiki/Missouri_tribe "Missouri tribe"), whose name in turn meant "people with wooden canoes"[[1]](#cite_note-GNIS-1)

[Great Falls, MT](/wiki/Great_Falls,_MT "Great Falls, MT"), [Bismarck, ND](/wiki/Bismarck,_ND "Bismarck, ND"), [Pierre, SD](/wiki/Pierre,_SD "Pierre, SD"), [Sioux City, IA](/wiki/Sioux_City,_IA "Sioux City, IA"), [Omaha, NE](/wiki/Omaha,_NE "Omaha, NE"), [Brownville, NE](/wiki/Brownville,_NE "Brownville, NE"), [Saint Joseph, MO](/wiki/Saint_Joseph,_MO "Saint Joseph, MO"), [Kansas City, MO-KS](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri")

[Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_River "Jefferson River"), [Dearborn](/wiki/Dearborn_River "Dearborn River"), [Sun](/wiki/Sun_River "Sun River"), [Marias](/wiki/Marias_River "Marias River"), [Milk](/wiki/Milk_River_(Alberta%E2%80%93Montana) "Milk River (Alberta–Montana)"), [James](/wiki/James_River_(Dakotas) "James River (Dakotas)"), [Big Sioux](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River"), [Grand](/wiki/Grand_River_(Missouri) "Grand River (Missouri)"), [Chariton](/wiki/Chariton_River "Chariton River")

[Madison](/wiki/Madison_River "Madison River"), [Gallatin](/wiki/Gallatin_River "Gallatin River"), [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_River "Yellowstone River"), [Little Missouri](/wiki/Little_Missouri_River_(North_Dakota) "Little Missouri River (North Dakota)"), [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne_River "Cheyenne River"), [Bad](/wiki/Bad_River_(South_Dakota) "Bad River (South Dakota)"), [White](/wiki/White_River_(Missouri_River) "White River (Missouri River)"), [Niobrara](/wiki/Niobrara_River "Niobrara River"), [Platte](/wiki/Platte_River "Platte River"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_River "Kansas River"), [Osage](/wiki/Osage_River "Osage River"), [Gasconade](/wiki/Gasconade_River "Gasconade River")

is a [river](/wiki/River "River") in the [Central](/wiki/Central_United_States "Central United States") and [Mountain West](/wiki/Mountain_states "Mountain states") regions of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). The nation's longest,[[13]](#cite_note-RiversWorld-13) it rises in the eastern [Centennial Mountains](/wiki/Centennial_Mountains "Centennial Mountains") of the [Bitterroot Range](/wiki/Bitterroot_Range "Bitterroot Range") of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of southwestern [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), then flows east and south for 2,342 mi (3,769 km)[[6]](#cite_note-modifications-6) before entering the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") north of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri. The river drains a [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of more than 500,000 sq mi (1,300,000 km2), which includes parts of ten U.S. states and two Canadian provinces. Although a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the Mississippi, the Missouri River is slightly longer[[14]](#cite_note-USGS-14) and carries a comparable volume of water.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7)[[15]](#cite_note-MississippiStLouis-15). A fellow tributary, the ([Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River")), carries more water.[[16]](#cite_note-16) When combined with the lower Mississippi River, it forms the [world's fourth-longest river system](/wiki/List_of_rivers_by_length "List of rivers by length").[[13]](#cite_note-RiversWorld-13)

The **Missouri River** is a [river](/wiki/River "River") in the [Central](/wiki/Central_United_States "Central United States") and [Mountain West](/wiki/Mountain_states "Mountain states") regions of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). The nation's longest,[[13]](#cite_note-RiversWorld-13) it rises in the eastern [Centennial Mountains](/wiki/Centennial_Mountains "Centennial Mountains") of the [Bitterroot Range](/wiki/Bitterroot_Range "Bitterroot Range") of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of southwestern [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), then flows east and south for 2,342 mi (3,769 km)[[6]](#cite_note-modifications-6) before entering the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") north of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri. The river drains a [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of more than 500,000 sq mi (1,300,000 km2), which includes parts of ten U.S. states and two Canadian provinces. Although a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the Mississippi, the Missouri River is slightly longer[[14]](#cite_note-USGS-14) and carries a comparable volume of water.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7)[[15]](#cite_note-MississippiStLouis-15). A fellow tributary, the ([Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River")), carries more water.[[16]](#cite_note-16) When combined with the lower Mississippi River, it forms the [world's fourth-longest river system](/wiki/List_of_rivers_by_length "List of rivers by length").[[13]](#cite_note-RiversWorld-13)

For over 12,000 years, people have depended on the Missouri River and its [tributaries](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") as a source of sustenance and transportation. More than ten major groups of [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") populated the watershed, with most leading a nomadic lifestyle and dependent on enormous [bison](/wiki/American_bison "American bison") herds that roamed through the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"). The first Europeans encountered the river in the late seventeenth century, and the region passed through Spanish and French hands before becoming part of the United States through the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase").

The Missouri River was one of the main routes for the westward expansion of the United States during the 19th century. The growth of the [fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade") in the early 19th century laid much of the groundwork as trappers explored the region and blazed trails. [Pioneers](/wiki/American_pioneer "American pioneer") headed west *en masse* beginning in the 1830s, first by [covered wagon](/wiki/Covered_wagon "Covered wagon"), then by the growing numbers of [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") that entered service on the river. Conflict between settlers and Native Americans in the watershed led to some of the most longstanding violence of the [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars#Great_Plains "American Indian Wars").

During the 20th century, the Missouri River basin was extensively developed for irrigation, flood control, and the generation of [hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"). Fifteen dams impound the [main stem](/wiki/Main_stem "Main stem") of the river, with hundreds more on tributaries. The Missouri River's reservoirs include the largest, second-largest, and fourth-[largest artificial lakes in the United States](/wiki/List_of_largest_reservoirs_in_the_United_States "List of largest reservoirs in the United States") by surface area: [Lake Sakakawea](/wiki/Lake_Sakakawea "Lake Sakakawea"), [Lake Oahe](/wiki/Lake_Oahe "Lake Oahe"), and [Fort Peck Lake](/wiki/Fort_Peck_Lake "Fort Peck Lake"). [Meanders](/wiki/Meander "Meander") have been cut off and the river [channelized](/wiki/Channelization_(river) "Channelization (river)") to improve navigation, reducing its length by almost 200 mi (320 km) from pre-development times. Although the lower [Missouri valley](/wiki/Missouri_River_Valley "Missouri River Valley") is now a populous and highly productive agricultural and industrial region, heavy development has taken its toll on wildlife and fish populations as well as water quality.

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Course")]

From the Rocky Mountains, three streams rise to form the headwaters of the Missouri River:

* The longest source stream begins near [Brower's Spring](/wiki/Brower%27s_Spring "Brower's Spring") in southwest [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), 9,100 feet (2,800 m) above sea level on the southeastern slopes of [Mount Jefferson](/wiki/Mount_Jefferson_(Bitterroot_Range) "Mount Jefferson (Bitterroot Range)") in the [Centennial Mountains](/wiki/Centennial_Mountains "Centennial Mountains"). From there it flows west then north; runs first in [Hell Roaring Creek](/wiki/Hell_Roaring_Creek "Hell Roaring Creek") then west into the [Red Rock](/wiki/Red_Rock_River_(Montana) "Red Rock River (Montana)"); swings northeast to become the [Beaverhead River](/wiki/Beaverhead_River "Beaverhead River"); and finally joins with the [Big Hole](/wiki/Big_Hole_River "Big Hole River") to form the [Jefferson River](/wiki/Jefferson_River "Jefferson River").
* The [Firehole River](/wiki/Firehole_River "Firehole River"), which originates in northwest [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") at [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park")'s Madison Lake, joins with the [Gibbon River](/wiki/Gibbon_River "Gibbon River") to form the [Madison River](/wiki/Madison_River "Madison River").
* The [Gallatin River](/wiki/Gallatin_River "Gallatin River") flows out of Gallatin Lake which is also in Yellowstone National Park.

[![View of a deep blue lake surrounded by low mountains](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Holter_Lake.jpg/250px-Holter_Lake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holter_Lake.jpg)

[Holter Lake](/wiki/Holter_Lake "Holter Lake"), a reservoir on the upper Missouri River

The Missouri River officially starts at the confluence of the Jefferson and Madison in [Missouri Headwaters State Park](/wiki/Missouri_Headwaters_State_Park "Missouri Headwaters State Park") near [Three Forks, Montana](/wiki/Three_Forks,_Montana "Three Forks, Montana"), and is joined by the Gallatin a mile (1.6 km) downstream. It then passes through [Canyon Ferry Lake](/wiki/Canyon_Ferry_Lake "Canyon Ferry Lake"), a reservoir west of the [Big Belt Mountains](/wiki/Big_Belt_Mountains "Big Belt Mountains"). Issuing from the mountains near [Cascade](/wiki/Cascade,_Montana "Cascade, Montana"), the river flows northeast to the city of [Great Falls](/wiki/Great_Falls,_Montana "Great Falls, Montana"), where it drops over the [Great Falls of the Missouri](/wiki/Great_Falls_(Missouri_River) "Great Falls (Missouri River)"), a series of five substantial waterfalls. It then winds east through a scenic region of canyons and badlands known as the Missouri Breaks, receiving the [Marias River](/wiki/Marias_River "Marias River") from the west then widening into the [Fort Peck Lake](/wiki/Fort_Peck_Lake "Fort Peck Lake") reservoir a few miles above the confluence with the [Musselshell River](/wiki/Musselshell_River "Musselshell River"). Farther on, the river passes through the [Fort Peck Dam](/wiki/Fort_Peck_Dam "Fort Peck Dam"), and immediately downstream, the [Milk River](/wiki/Milk_River_(Alberta%E2%80%93Montana) "Milk River (Alberta–Montana)") joins from the north.[[17]](#cite_note-topoquest-17)[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18)

Flowing eastward through the plains of eastern Montana, the Missouri receives the [Poplar River](/wiki/Poplar_River_(Montana%E2%80%93Saskatchewan) "Poplar River (Montana–Saskatchewan)") from the north before crossing into [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") where the [Yellowstone River](/wiki/Yellowstone_River "Yellowstone River"), its greatest tributary by volume, joins from the southwest. At the confluence, the Yellowstone is actually the larger river.[[a]](#cite_note-21)
The Missouri then meanders east past [Williston](/wiki/Williston,_North_Dakota "Williston, North Dakota") and into [Lake Sakakawea](/wiki/Lake_Sakakawea "Lake Sakakawea"), the reservoir formed by [Garrison Dam](/wiki/Garrison_Dam "Garrison Dam"). Below the dam the Missouri receives the [Knife River](/wiki/Knife_River "Knife River") from the west and flows south to [Bismarck](/wiki/Bismarck,_North_Dakota "Bismarck, North Dakota"), the capital of North Dakota, where the [Heart River](/wiki/Heart_River_(North_Dakota) "Heart River (North Dakota)") joins from the west. It slows into the [Lake Oahe](/wiki/Lake_Oahe "Lake Oahe") reservoir just before the [Cannonball River](/wiki/Cannonball_River "Cannonball River") confluence. While it continues south, eventually reaching [Oahe Dam](/wiki/Oahe_Dam "Oahe Dam") in [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), the [Grand](/wiki/Grand_River_(South_Dakota) "Grand River (South Dakota)"), [Moreau](/wiki/Moreau_River_(South_Dakota) "Moreau River (South Dakota)") and [Cheyenne Rivers](/wiki/Cheyenne_River "Cheyenne River") all join the Missouri from the west.[[17]](#cite_note-topoquest-17)[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18)

The Missouri makes a bend to the southeast as it winds through the Great Plains, receiving the [Niobrara River](/wiki/Niobrara_River "Niobrara River") and many smaller tributaries from the southwest. It then proceeds to form the boundary of South Dakota and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") and is joined by the [James River](/wiki/James_River_(Dakotas) "James River (Dakotas)") from the north. At [Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City "Sioux City") the [Big Sioux River](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") comes in from the north, after which the Missouri forms the [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa")–Nebraska boundary. It flows south to the city of [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska") where it receives its longest tributary, the [Platte River](/wiki/Platte_River "Platte River"), from the west.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Downstream, it begins to define the border between the states of Nebraska and [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), then flows between the states of Missouri and [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"). The Missouri swings east at [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), where the [Kansas River](/wiki/Kansas_River "Kansas River") enters from the west, and so on into north-central Missouri. To the east of Kansas City, the Missouri receives, on the left side, the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Missouri) "Grand River (Missouri)"). It passes south of [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia,_Missouri "Columbia, Missouri") and receives the [Osage](/wiki/Osage_River "Osage River") and [Gasconade](/wiki/Gasconade_River "Gasconade River") Rivers from the south downstream of [Jefferson City](/wiki/Jefferson_City,_Missouri "Jefferson City, Missouri"). The river then rounds the northern side of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri") to join the Mississippi River on the border between Missouri and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois").[[17]](#cite_note-topoquest-17)[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18)

## Watershed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Watershed")]

> There is only one river with a personality, a sense of humor, and a woman's caprice; a river that goes traveling sidewise, that interferes in politics, rearranges geography, and dabbles in real estate; a river that plays hide and seek with you today and tomorrow follows you around like a pet dog with a dynamite cracker tied to his tail. That river is the Missouri.
>
> — George Fitch[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The Missouri River's [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") spans 529,350 square miles (1,371,000 km2),[[12]](#cite_note-Story-12)
encompassing nearly one-sixth of the area of the United States[[23]](#cite_note-24) or just over five percent of the continent of North America.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Comparable to the size of the Canadian province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the watershed covers most of the central Great Plains, stretching from the Rocky Mountains in the west to the [Mississippi River Valley](/wiki/Mississippi_River_Valley "Mississippi River Valley") in the east and from the southern extreme of western Canada to the border of the [Arkansas River](/wiki/Arkansas_River "Arkansas River") watershed. Compared with the Mississippi River above their confluence, the Missouri is twice as long[[b]](#cite_note-26)
and drains an area three times as large.[[c]](#cite_note-27)
The Missouri accounts for 45 percent of the annual flow of the Mississippi past St. Louis, and as much as 70 percent in certain droughts.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7)[[15]](#cite_note-MississippiStLouis-15)

In 1990, the Missouri River watershed was home to about 12 million people.[[12]](#cite_note-Story-12)[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) This included the entire population of the U.S. state of Nebraska, parts of the U.S. states of Colorado, Iowa, Kansas, Minnesota, Missouri, Montana, North Dakota, South Dakota, and Wyoming, and small southern portions of the Canadian provinces of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan").[[12]](#cite_note-Story-12) The watershed's largest city is [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), Colorado, with a population of more than six hundred thousand. Denver is the main city of the [Front Range Urban Corridor](/wiki/Front_Range_Urban_Corridor "Front Range Urban Corridor") whose cities had a combined population of over four million in 2005,[[26]](#cite_note-29) making it the largest metropolitan area in the Missouri River basin.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) Other major population centers – mostly in the watershed's southeastern portion – include [Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), north of the confluence of the Missouri and Platte Rivers; [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") – [Kansas City, Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Kansas "Kansas City, Kansas"), at the confluence of the Missouri with the Kansas River; and the St. Louis metropolitan area, south of the Missouri River just below the latter's mouth, on the Mississippi.[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18) In contrast, the northwestern part of the watershed is sparsely populated. However, many northwestern cities, such as [Billings, Montana](/wiki/Billings,_Montana "Billings, Montana"), are among the fastest growing in the Missouri basin.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)

With more than 170,000 square miles (440,000 km2) under the plow, the Missouri River watershed includes roughly one-fourth of all the agricultural land in the United States, providing more than a third of the country's wheat, flax, barley, and oats. However, only 11,000 square miles (28,000 km2) of farmland in the basin is irrigated. A further 281,000 square miles (730,000 km2) of the basin is devoted to the raising of livestock, mainly cattle. Forested areas of the watershed, mostly [second-growth](/wiki/Second-growth_forest "Second-growth forest"), total about 43,700 square miles (113,000 km2). Urban areas, on the other hand, comprise less than 13,000 square miles (34,000 km2) of land. Most built-up areas are along the main stem and a few major tributaries, including the Platte and Yellowstone Rivers.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)[[27]](#cite_note-30)

[![The sun low over the horizon over a body of water surrounded by dark vegetation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Missouri-bismarck-nd-2004-1.jpg/250px-Missouri-bismarck-nd-2004-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouri-bismarck-nd-2004-1.jpg)

The Missouri in [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), which was the furthest upstream that French explorers traveled on the river

Elevation in the watershed varies from just over 400 feet (120 m) at the Missouri's mouth[[1]](#cite_note-GNIS-1) to the 14,293-foot (4,357 m) summit of [Mount Lincoln](/wiki/Mount_Lincoln_(Colorado) "Mount Lincoln (Colorado)") in central Colorado.[[28]](#cite_note-31)[[29]](#cite_note-32) The river drops 8,626 feet (2,629 m) from Brower's Spring, the farthest source. Although the plains of the watershed have extremely little local vertical relief, the land rises about 10 feet per mile (1.9 m/km) from east to west. The elevation is less than 500 feet (150 m) at the eastern border of the watershed, but is over 3,000 feet (910 m) above sea level in many places at the base of the Rockies.[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18)

The Missouri's drainage basin has highly variable weather and rainfall patterns, Overall, the watershed is defined by a [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") with warm, wet summers and harsh, cold winters. Most of the watershed receives an average of 8 to 10 inches (200 to 250 mm) of precipitation each year.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) However, the westernmost portions of the basin in the Rockies as well as southeastern regions in Missouri may receive as much as 40 inches (1,000 mm).[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) The vast majority of precipitation occurs in summer in most of the lower and middle basin, although the upper basin is known for short-lived but intense summer [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") such as the one which produced the [1972 Black Hills flood](/wiki/Black_Hills_flood "Black Hills flood") through [Rapid City, South Dakota](/wiki/Rapid_City,_South_Dakota "Rapid City, South Dakota").[[30]](#cite_note-33) Winter temperatures in the northern and western portions of the basin typically drop to −20 °F (−28.9 °C) or lower every winter with extremes as low as −60 °F (−51.1 °C), while summer highs occasionally exceed 100 °F (37.8 °C) in all areas except the higher elevations of Montana, Wyoming and Colorado. Extreme maxima — almost all prior to 1960 — have exceeded 115 °F (46.1 °C) in all US states in the basin.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)[[31]](#cite_note-34)

As one of the continent's most significant river systems,[[32]](#cite_note-watershedmap-35) the Missouri's drainage basin borders on many other major watersheds of the United States and Canada. The [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas"), running along the spine of the Rocky Mountains, forms most of the western border of the Missouri watershed.[[32]](#cite_note-watershedmap-35) The [Clark Fork](/wiki/Clark_Fork_(river) "Clark Fork (river)") and [Snake River](/wiki/Snake_River "Snake River"), both part of the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") basin, drain the area west of the Rockies in Montana, [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") and western Wyoming. The Columbia, Missouri and [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") watersheds meet at [Three Waters Mountain](/wiki/Three_Waters_Mountain "Three Waters Mountain") in Wyoming's [Wind River Range](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range").[[33]](#cite_note-36) South of there, the Missouri basin is bordered on the west by the drainage of the [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)"), a tributary of the Colorado, then on the south by the mainstem of the Colorado. Both the Colorado and Columbia Rivers flow to the Pacific Ocean. However, a large [endorheic drainage](/wiki/Endorheic "Endorheic") called the [Great Divide Basin](/wiki/Great_Divide_Basin "Great Divide Basin") exists between the Missouri and Green watersheds in western Wyoming. This area is sometimes counted as part of the Missouri River watershed, even though its waters do not flow to either side of the Continental Divide.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

To the north, the much lower [Laurentian Divide](/wiki/Laurentian_Divide "Laurentian Divide") separates the Missouri River watershed from those of the [Oldman River](/wiki/Oldman_River "Oldman River"), a tributary of the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River"), as well as the [Souris](/wiki/Souris_River "Souris River"), [Sheyenne](/wiki/Sheyenne_River "Sheyenne River"), and smaller tributaries of the [Red River of the North](/wiki/Red_River_of_the_North "Red River of the North"). All of these streams are part of Canada's [Nelson River](/wiki/Nelson_River "Nelson River") drainage basin, which empties into [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"). There are also several large endorheic basins between the Missouri and Nelson watersheds in southern Alberta and Saskatchewan.[[32]](#cite_note-watershedmap-35) The [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_River "Minnesota River") and [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines_River "Des Moines River") Rivers, tributaries of the upper Mississippi, drain most of the area bordering the eastern side of the Missouri River basin. Finally, on the south, the [Ozark Mountains](/wiki/The_Ozarks "The Ozarks") and other low divides through central Missouri, Kansas and Colorado separate the Missouri watershed from those of the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Arkansas) "White River (Arkansas)") and Arkansas River, also tributaries of the Mississippi River.[[32]](#cite_note-watershedmap-35)

### Major tributaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major tributaries")]

[![A river flows past grass-covered banks, trees are in the midground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Yellowstone_River%2C_flowing_through_Paradise_Valley.jpg/330px-Yellowstone_River%2C_flowing_through_Paradise_Valley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yellowstone_River,_flowing_through_Paradise_Valley.jpg)

The [Yellowstone River](/wiki/Yellowstone_River "Yellowstone River"), the fifth longest tributary of the Missouri, which it joins in North Dakota

Main article: [List of tributaries of the Missouri River](/wiki/List_of_tributaries_of_the_Missouri_River "List of tributaries of the Missouri River")

Over 95 significant tributaries and hundreds of smaller ones feed the Missouri River, with most of the larger ones coming in as the river draws close to the mouth.[[35]](#cite_note-38) Most rivers and streams in the Missouri River basin flow from west to east, following the incline of the Great Plains; however, some eastern tributaries such as the James, [Big Sioux](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") and [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Missouri) "Grand River (Missouri)") systems flow from north to south.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)

The Missouri's largest tributaries by runoff are the Yellowstone in Montana and Wyoming, the Platte in Wyoming, Colorado, and Nebraska, and the [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_River "Kansas River")–[Republican](/wiki/Republican_River "Republican River")/[Smoky Hill](/wiki/Smoky_Hill_River "Smoky Hill River") and Osage in Kansas and Missouri. Each of these tributaries drains an area greater than 50,000 square miles (130,000 km2) or has an average discharge greater than 5,000 cu ft/s (140 m3/s).[[14]](#cite_note-USGS-14)[[36]](#cite_note-USGSrivers-39) The Yellowstone River has the highest discharge, even though the Platte is longer and drains a larger area. In fact, the Yellowstone's flow is about 13,800 cu ft/s (390 m3/s)[[37]](#cite_note-Yellowstoneflow-40) – accounting for sixteen percent of total runoff in the Missouri basin and nearly double that of the Platte.[[38]](#cite_note-Platteflow-41) On the other end of the scale is the tiny [Roe River](/wiki/Roe_River "Roe River") in Montana, which at 201 feet (61 m) long is one of the world's shortest rivers.[[39]](#cite_note-42)

The table on the right lists the ten longest tributaries of the Missouri, along with their respective catchment areas and flows. Length is measured to the hydrologic source, regardless of naming convention. The main stem of the Kansas River, for example, is 148 miles (238 km) long.[[40]](#cite_note-NationalMap-43) However, including the longest headwaters tributaries, the 453-mile (729 km) Republican River and the 156-mile (251 km) [Arikaree River](/wiki/Arikaree_River "Arikaree River"), brings the total length to 749 miles (1,205 km).[[40]](#cite_note-NationalMap-43) Similar naming issues are encountered with the Platte River, whose longest tributary, the [North Platte River](/wiki/North_Platte_River "North Platte River"), is more than twice as long as its mainstream.[[40]](#cite_note-NationalMap-43)

The Missouri's headwaters above Three Forks extend much farther upstream than the main stem. Measured to the farthest source at Brower's Spring, the Jefferson River is 298 miles (480 km) long.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) Thus measured to its highest headwaters, the Missouri River stretches for 2,639 miles (4,247 km). When combined with the lower Mississippi, the Missouri and its headwaters form part of the [fourth-longest river system in the world](/wiki/List_of_rivers_by_length "List of rivers by length"), at 3,745 miles (6,027 km).[[10]](#cite_note-headwaters-10)

### Discharge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Discharge")]

[![Aerial view of farms and a power station in a rural area partly inundated by a river that has overflowed its banks](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Corp_of_Eng._6-16-11A_267.JPG/250px-Corp_of_Eng._6-16-11A_267.JPG)](/wiki/File:Corp_of_Eng._6-16-11A_267.JPG)

Nebraska's [Fort Calhoun Nuclear Generating Station](/wiki/Fort_Calhoun_Nuclear_Generating_Station "Fort Calhoun Nuclear Generating Station") was inundated when the Missouri River [flooded in 2011](/wiki/2011_Missouri_River_Floods "2011 Missouri River Floods")

By [discharge](/wiki/Discharge_(hydrology) "Discharge (hydrology)"), the Missouri is the ninth largest river of the United States, after the Mississippi, [St. Lawrence](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), Columbia, [Niagara](/wiki/Niagara_River "Niagara River"), [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon_River "Yukon River"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_River "Detroit River"), and [St. Clair](/wiki/St._Clair_River "St. Clair River"). The latter two, however, are sometimes considered part of a strait between [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron") and [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie").[[50]](#cite_note-USrivers-53) Among rivers of North America as a whole, the Missouri is thirteenth largest, after the Mississippi, [Mackenzie](/wiki/Mackenzie_River "Mackenzie River"), St. Lawrence, Ohio, Columbia, Niagara, Yukon, Detroit, St. Clair, [Fraser](/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River"), [Slave](/wiki/Slave_River "Slave River"), and [Koksoak](/wiki/Koksoak_River "Koksoak River").[[50]](#cite_note-USrivers-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54)

As the Missouri drains a predominantly semi-arid region, its discharge is much lower and more variable than other North American rivers of comparable length. Before the construction of dams, the river flooded twice each year – once in the "April Rise" or "[Spring Fresh](/wiki/Freshet "Freshet")", with the melting of snow on the plains of the watershed, and in the "June Rise", caused by snowmelt and summer rainstorms in the Rocky Mountains. The latter was far more destructive, with the river increasing to over ten times its normal discharge in some years.[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56) The Missouri's discharge is affected by over 17,000 reservoirs with an aggregate capacity of some 141 million acre-feet (174 km3).[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) By providing flood control, the reservoirs dramatically reduce peak flows and increase low flows. Evaporation from reservoirs significantly reduces the river's runoff, causing an annual loss of over 3 million acre-feet (3.7 km3) from mainstem reservoirs alone.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)
