The [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") operates fifty-one [stream gauges](/wiki/Stream_gauge "Stream gauge") along the Missouri River. The river's average discharge at Bismarck, 1,314.5 miles (2,115.5 km) from the mouth, is 21,920 cu ft/s (621 m3/s). This is from a drainage area of 186,400 mi2 (483,000 km2), or 35% of the total river basin.[[61]](#cite_note-Bismarckdischarge-64) At Kansas City, 366.1 miles (589.2 km) from the mouth, the river's average flow is 55,400 cu ft/s (1,570 m3/s). The river here drains about 484,100 mi2 (1,254,000 km2), representing about 91% of the entire basin.[[55]](#cite_note-KSCdischarge-58)

The lowermost gage with a period of record greater than fifty years is at [Hermann, Missouri](/wiki/Hermann,_Missouri "Hermann, Missouri") – 97.9 miles (157.6 km) upstream of the mouth of the Missouri – where the average annual flow was 87,520 cu ft/s (2,478 m3/s) from 1897 to 2010. About 522,500 mi2 (1,353,000 km2), or 98.7% of the watershed, lies above Hermann.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7) The highest annual mean was 181,800 cu ft/s (5,150 m3/s) in 1993, and the lowest was 41,690 cu ft/s (1,181 m3/s) in 2006.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7) Extremes of the flow vary even further. The largest discharge ever recorded was over 750,000 cu ft/s (21,000 m3/s) on July 31, 1993, during [a historic flood](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1993 "Great Flood of 1993").[[62]](#cite_note-65) The lowest, a mere 602 cu ft/s (17.0 m3/s) – caused by the formation of an [ice dam](/wiki/Ice_jam "Ice jam") – was measured on December 23, 1963.[[7]](#cite_note-missouriflow-7)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![Top down view of two rivers merging, one dark and clear and the other light with clouds of sediment](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Missouri-Mississippi_confluence.jpg/250px-Missouri-Mississippi_confluence.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouri-Mississippi_confluence.jpg)

High [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt") content makes the Missouri River (left) noticeably lighter than the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") (right) at their confluence north of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri").

The Rocky Mountains of southwestern Montana at the [headwaters](/wiki/Headwaters "Headwaters") of the Missouri River first rose in the [Laramide Orogeny](/wiki/Laramide_Orogeny "Laramide Orogeny"), a [mountain-building](/wiki/Mountain_formation "Mountain formation") episode that occurred from around 70 to 45 million years ago (the end of the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") through the early [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic")).[[63]](#cite_note-cenozoic_map-66) This [orogeny](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny") [uplifted](/wiki/Tectonic_uplift "Tectonic uplift") [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") rocks along the western side of the [Western Interior Seaway](/wiki/Western_Interior_Seaway "Western Interior Seaway"), a vast shallow sea that stretched from the Arctic Ocean to the Gulf of Mexico, and deposited the sediments that now underlie much of the drainage basin of the Missouri River.[[64]](#cite_note-67)[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69)
This Laramide uplift caused the sea to retreat and laid the framework for a vast drainage system of rivers flowing from the Rocky and [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), the predecessor of the modern-day Mississippi watershed.[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) The Laramide Orogeny is essential to modern Missouri River [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology"), as snow and ice melt from the Rockies provide the majority of the flow in the Missouri and its tributaries.[[70]](#cite_note-73)

The Missouri and many of its tributaries cross the Great Plains, flowing over or cutting into the [Ogallala Group](/wiki/Ogallala_Aquifer "Ogallala Aquifer") and older mid-Cenozoic sedimentary rocks. The lowest major Cenozoic unit, the [White River Formation](/wiki/White_River_Formation "White River Formation"), was deposited between roughly 35 and 29 million years ago[[71]](#cite_note-laramide_cliamte-74)[[72]](#cite_note-wyoming_white_river-75) and consists of [claystone](/wiki/Claystone "Claystone"), [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), and [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(geology) "Conglomerate (geology)").[[72]](#cite_note-wyoming_white_river-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76) Channel sandstones and finer-grained [overbank deposits](/wiki/Overbank_deposit "Overbank deposit") of the [fluvial](/wiki/Fluvial "Fluvial")[[74]](#cite_note-gs_arikaree-77) [Arikaree Group](/wiki/Arikaree_Group "Arikaree Group") were deposited between 29 and 19 million years ago.[[71]](#cite_note-laramide_cliamte-74) The [Miocene](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene")-age Ogallala and the slightly younger [Pliocene](/wiki/Pliocene "Pliocene")-age Broadwater Formation deposited atop the Arikaree Group, and are formed from material eroded off of the Rocky Mountains during a time of increased generation of topographic relief;[[71]](#cite_note-laramide_cliamte-74)[[75]](#cite_note-78) these formations stretch from the Rocky Mountains nearly to the Iowa border and give the Great Plains much of their gentle but persistent eastward tilt, and also constitute a major aquifer.[[76]](#cite_note-79)

Immediately before the [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") [Ice Age](/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation"), the Missouri River was likely split into three segments: an upper portion that drained northwards into Hudson Bay,[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81)
and middle and lower sections that flowed eastward down the regional slope.[[79]](#cite_note-82)
As the Earth plunged into the Ice Age, a [pre-Illinoian](/wiki/Pre-Illinoian "Pre-Illinoian") (or possibly the [Illinoian](/wiki/Illinoian "Illinoian")) [glaciation](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period") diverted the Missouri River southeastward toward its present confluence with the Mississippi and caused it to integrate into a single river system that cuts across the regional slope.[[80]](#cite_note-83) In western Montana, the Missouri River is thought to have once flowed north then east around the [Bear Paw Mountains](/wiki/Bear_Paw_Mountains "Bear Paw Mountains"). [Sapphires](/wiki/Sapphire "Sapphire") are found in some spots along the river in western Montana.[[81]](#cite_note-gemgallerymtloc-84)[[82]](#cite_note-85) Advances of the continental ice sheets diverted the river and its tributaries, causing them to pool up into large temporary lakes such as Glacial Lakes [Great Falls](/wiki/Glacial_Lake_Great_Falls "Glacial Lake Great Falls"), Musselshell and others. As the lakes rose, the water in them often spilled across adjacent local drainage divides, creating now-abandoned channels and [coulees](/wiki/Coulee "Coulee") including the [Shonkin Sag](/wiki/Shonkin_Sag "Shonkin Sag"), 100 miles (160 km) long. When the glaciers retreated, the Missouri flowed in a new course along the south side of the Bearpaws, and the lower part of the Milk River tributary took over the original main channel.[[83]](#cite_note-86)

The Missouri's nickname, the "Big Muddy", was inspired by its enormous loads of [sediment](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") or silt – some of the largest of any North American river.[[2]](#cite_note-Spotlight-2)[[84]](#cite_note-87) In its pre-development state, the river [transported](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") some 175 to 320 million short tons (159 to 290 million metric tons) per year.[[85]](#cite_note-88) The construction of dams and levees has drastically reduced this to 20 to 25 million short tons (18 to 23 million metric tons) in the present day.[[86]](#cite_note-sediment-management-89) Much of this sediment is derived from the river's [floodplain](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain"), also called the meander belt; every time the river changed course, it would erode tons of soil and rocks from its banks. However, damming and channeling the river has kept it from reaching its natural sediment sources along most of its course. Reservoirs along the Missouri trap roughly 36.4 million short tons (33.0 million metric tons) of sediment each year.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) Despite this, the river still transports more than half the total silt that empties into the Gulf of Mexico; the [Mississippi River Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_River_Delta "Mississippi River Delta"), formed by sediment deposits at the mouth of the Mississippi, constitutes a majority of sediments carried by the Missouri.[[86]](#cite_note-sediment-management-89)[[87]](#cite_note-90)

## First people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First people")]

See also: [Plains Indians](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians")

Archaeological evidence, especially in Missouri, suggests that human beings first inhabited the watershed of the Missouri River between 10,000 and 12,000 years ago at the end of the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene").[[88]](#cite_note-archaeology-91) During the end of the [last glacial period](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period"), large migration of humans were taking place, such as those via the [Bering land bridge](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") between the Americas and Eurasia. Over centuries, the Missouri River formed one of these main migration paths. Most migratory groups that passed through the area eventually settled in the [Ohio Valley](/wiki/Ohio_Valley "Ohio Valley") and the lower Mississippi River Valley, but many, including the [Mound builders](/wiki/Mound_builder_(people) "Mound builder (people)"), stayed along the Missouri, becoming the ancestors of the later Indigenous peoples of the Great Plains.[[89]](#cite_note-92)

[![Painting showing a village on a bluff above a river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/A_Mandan_village_0049v_cropped.jpg/250px-A_Mandan_village_0049v_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Mandan_village_0049v_cropped.jpg)

[Karl Bodmer](/wiki/Karl_Bodmer "Karl Bodmer"), *A Mandan Village*, c. 1840–1843

[Indigenous peoples of North America](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_North_America "Indigenous peoples of North America") who have lived along the Missouri have historically had access to ample food, water, and shelter. Many migratory animals naturally inhabit the plains area. Before they were hunted by colonists and Native Americans, these animals, such as the [buffalo](/wiki/American_bison "American bison"), provided meat, clothing, and other everyday items; there were also great [riparian](/wiki/Riparian_zone "Riparian zone") areas in the river's floodplain that provided habitat for herbs and other staple foods.[[90]](#cite_note-93) No written records from the tribes and peoples of the pre-European contact period exist because they did not yet use writing. According to the writings of early colonists, some of the major tribes along the Missouri River included the [Otoe](/wiki/Otoe_tribe "Otoe tribe"), [Missouria](/wiki/Missouria "Missouria"), [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_(tribe) "Omaha (tribe)"), [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca"), [Dakota](/wiki/Dakota_people "Dakota people"), [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people"), [Arikara](/wiki/Arikara "Arikara"), [Hidatsa](/wiki/Hidatsa "Hidatsa"), [Mandan](/wiki/Mandan "Mandan"), [Assiniboine](/wiki/Assiniboine_people "Assiniboine people"), [Gros Ventres](/wiki/Gros_Ventres "Gros Ventres") and [Blackfeet](/wiki/Blackfoot_Confederacy "Blackfoot Confederacy").[[91]](#cite_note-94)

In this pre-colonial and early-colonial era, the Missouri river was used as a path of trade and transport, and the river and its tributaries often formed territorial boundaries. Most of the Indigenous peoples in the region at that time had semi-nomadic cultures, with many tribes maintaining different summer and winter camps. However, the center of Native American wealth and trade lay along the Missouri River in the Dakotas region on its great bend south.[[92]](#cite_note-95) A large cluster of walled Mandan, Hidatsa and Arikara villages situated on bluffs and islands of the river was home to thousands, and later served as a market and trading post used by early French and British explorers and fur traders.[[93]](#cite_note-96) Following the introduction of horses to Missouri River tribes, possibly from feral European-introduced populations, Natives' way of life changed dramatically. The use of the horse allowed them to travel greater distances, and thus facilitated hunting, communications, warfare, the Siouxoan genocide and expulsion of many tribes, and the abundance of trade.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

Once, tens of millions of [American bison](/wiki/American_bison "American bison") (commonly called buffalo), one of the [keystone species](/wiki/Keystone_species "Keystone species") of the Great Plains and the Ohio Valley, roamed the plains of the Missouri River basin.[[95]](#cite_note-98) Most Native American nations in the basin relied heavily on the bison as a food source, and their hides and bones served to create other household items. In time, the species came to benefit from the indigenous peoples' periodic controlled burnings of the grasslands surrounding the Missouri to clear out old and dead growth. The large bison population of the region gave rise to the term *[great bison belt](/wiki/Great_bison_belt "Great bison belt")*, an area of rich annual grasslands that extended from [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") to Mexico along the eastern flank of the Continental Divide.[[96]](#cite_note-99) However, after the arrival of Europeans in North America, both the bison and the Native Americans saw a rapid decline in population.[[97]](#cite_note-100) Massive over-hunting for sport by colonists eliminated bison populations east of the Mississippi River by 1833 and reduced the numbers in the Missouri basin to a mere few hundred. Foreign diseases brought by settlers, such as [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), raged across the land, decimating Native American populations. Left without their primary source of sustenance, many of the remaining indigenous people were forced onto resettlement areas and reservations, often at gunpoint.[[98]](#cite_note-101)

## Early European explorers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early European explorers")]

[![Painting of a group of Native Americans surrounding and fighting with explorers](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/PawneeVillasur1720.jpg/250px-PawneeVillasur1720.jpg)](/wiki/File:PawneeVillasur1720.jpg)

Massacre of the [Villasur expedition](/wiki/Villasur_expedition "Villasur expedition"), painted c. 1720

In May 1673, the French-Canadian explorer [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") and the French explorer [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") left the settlement of [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan") on Lake Huron and traveled down the [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin_River "Wisconsin River") and Mississippi Rivers, aiming to reach the Pacific Ocean. In late June, Jolliet and Marquette became the first documented European discoverers of the Missouri River, which according to their journals was in full flood.[[99]](#cite_note-102) "I never saw anything more terrific," Jolliet wrote, "a tangle of entire trees from the mouth of the Pekistanoui [Missouri] with such impetuosity that one could not attempt to cross it without great danger. The commotion was such that the water was made muddy by it and could not clear itself."[[100]](#cite_note-Houck-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) They recorded *Pekitanoui* or *Pekistanoui* as the local name for the Missouri. However, the party never explored the Missouri beyond its mouth, nor did they linger in the area. In addition, they later learned that the Mississippi drained into the Gulf of Mexico and not the Pacific as they had originally presumed; the expedition turned back about 440 miles (710 km) short of the Gulf at the confluence of the Arkansas River with the Mississippi.[[100]](#cite_note-Houck-103)

In 1682, France expanded its territorial claims in North America to include land on the western side of the Mississippi River, which included the lower portion of the Missouri. However, the Missouri itself remained formally unexplored until [Étienne de Veniard, Sieur de Bourgmont](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_de_Veniard,_Sieur_de_Bourgmont "Étienne de Veniard, Sieur de Bourgmont") commanded an expedition in 1714 that reached at least as far as the mouth of the Platte River. It is unclear exactly how far Bourgmont traveled beyond there; he described the blond-haired [Mandans](/wiki/Mandan "Mandan") in his journals, so it is likely he reached as far as their villages in present-day North Dakota.[[102]](#cite_note-105) Later that year, Bourgmont published *The Route To Be Taken To Ascend The Missouri River*, the first known document to use the name "Missouri River"; many of the names he gave to tributaries, mostly from the native tribes that lived along them, are still in use today. The expedition's discoveries eventually found their way to cartographer [Guillaume Delisle](/wiki/Guillaume_Delisle "Guillaume Delisle"), who used the information to create a map of the lower Missouri.[[103]](#cite_note-Bourgmont-106) In 1718, [Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Le_Moyne,_Sieur_de_Bienville "Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville") requested that the French government bestow upon Bourgmont the [Cross of St. Louis](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Louis "Order of Saint Louis") because of his "outstanding service to France".[[103]](#cite_note-Bourgmont-106)

Bourgmont had in fact been in trouble with the French colonial authorities since 1706, when he deserted his post as commandant of [Fort Detroit](/wiki/Fort_Detroit "Fort Detroit") after poorly handling an [attack](/wiki/Siege_of_Fort_Detroit "Siege of Fort Detroit") by the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_(tribe) "Ottawa (tribe)") that resulted in thirty-one deaths.[[104]](#cite_note-107) However, his reputation was enhanced in 1720 when the [Pawnee](/wiki/Pawnee_people "Pawnee people") – who had earlier been befriended by Bourgmont – massacred the Spanish [Villasur expedition](/wiki/Villasur_expedition "Villasur expedition") near present-day [Columbus, Nebraska](/wiki/Columbus,_Nebraska "Columbus, Nebraska"), on the Missouri River, temporarily ending Spanish encroachment on French Louisiana.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

Bourgmont established [Fort Orleans](/wiki/Fort_Orleans "Fort Orleans"), the first European settlement of any kind on the Missouri River, near present-day [Brunswick, Missouri](/wiki/Brunswick,_Missouri "Brunswick, Missouri"), in 1723. The following year Bourgmont led an expedition to enlist [Comanche](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche") support against the Spanish, who continued to show interest in taking over the Missouri. In 1725 Bourgmont brought the chiefs of several Missouri River tribes to visit France. There he was raised to the rank of nobility and did not accompany the chiefs back to North America. Fort Orleans was either abandoned or its small contingent massacred by Native Americans in 1726.[[103]](#cite_note-Bourgmont-106)[[106]](#cite_note-109)

The [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") erupted when territorial disputes between France and Great Britain in North America reached a head in 1754. By 1763, France's army in North America had been defeated by a combined British-American force and was forced to sue for peace. In the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), France ceded its Canadian possessions to the British, gaining Louisiana from the [Spanish](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700%E2%80%931810) "History of Spain (1700–1810)") in return.[[107]](#cite_note-110)
Initially, the Spanish did not extensively explore the Missouri and let French traders continue their activities under license. However, this ended after news of incursions by trappers working for the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") in the upper Missouri River watershed was brought back following an expedition by Jacques D'Eglise in the early 1790s.[[108]](#cite_note-111) In 1795 the Spanish chartered the Company of Discoverers and Explorers of the Missouri, popularly referred to as the "Missouri Company", and offered a reward for the first person to reach the Pacific Ocean via the Missouri. In 1794 and 1795 expeditions led by [Jean-Baptiste Truteau](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Baptiste_Truteau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean-Baptiste Truteau (page does not exist)") and Antoine Simon Lecuyer de la Jonchšre did not even make it as far north as the Mandan villages in central North Dakota.[[109]](#cite_note-Evans-112)

Arguably the most successful of the Missouri Company expeditions was that of James MacKay and [John Evans](/wiki/John_Evans_(explorer) "John Evans (explorer)").[[110]](#cite_note-113) The two set out along the Missouri, and established Fort Charles about 20 miles (32 km) south of present-day Sioux City as a winter camp in 1795. At the Mandan villages in North Dakota, they forcefully expelled several British traders, and while talking to the populace they pinpointed the location of the Yellowstone River, which was called *Roche Jaune* ("Yellow Rock") by the French. Although MacKay and Evans failed to accomplish their original goal of reaching the Pacific, they did create the first accurate map of the upper Missouri River.[[109]](#cite_note-Evans-112)[[111]](#cite_note-114)

In 1795, the young United States and Spain signed [Pinckney's Treaty](/wiki/Pinckney%27s_Treaty "Pinckney's Treaty"), which recognized American rights to navigate the Mississippi River and store goods for export in New Orleans.[[112]](#cite_note-115) Three years later, Spain revoked the treaty and in 1800 secretly returned Louisiana to [Napoleonic](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") France in the [Third Treaty of San Ildefonso](/wiki/Third_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "Third Treaty of San Ildefonso"). This transfer was so secret that the Spanish continued to administer the territory. In 1801, Spain restored rights to use the Mississippi and New Orleans to the United States.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

[![An early map of western North America](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Map_of_Lewis_and_Clark%27s_Track%2C_Across_the_Western_Portion_of_North_America%2C_published_1814.jpg/500px-Map_of_Lewis_and_Clark%27s_Track%2C_Across_the_Western_Portion_of_North_America%2C_published_1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Lewis_and_Clark%27s_Track,_Across_the_Western_Portion_of_North_America,_published_1814.jpg)

Map of western North America drawn by [Lewis and Clark](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition")

Fearing that the cutoffs could occur again, President [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") proposed to buy the port of New Orleans from France for $10 million. Instead, faced with a debt crisis, Napoleon offered to sell the entirety of Louisiana, including the Missouri River, for $15 million – amounting to less than 3¢ per acre. The deal was signed in 1803, doubling the size of the United States with the acquisition of the [Louisiana Territory](/wiki/Louisiana_Territory "Louisiana Territory").[[114]](#cite_note-117)
In 1803, Jefferson instructed [Meriwether Lewis](/wiki/Meriwether_Lewis "Meriwether Lewis") to explore the Missouri and search for a water route to the Pacific Ocean. By then, it had been discovered that the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") system, which drains into the Pacific, had a similar latitude as the headwaters of the Missouri River, and it was widely believed that a connection or short portage existed between the two.[[115]](#cite_note-lewis1-118) However, Spain balked at the takeover, citing that they had never formally returned Louisiana to the French. Spanish authorities warned Lewis not to take the journey and forbade him from seeing the MacKay and Evans map of the Missouri, although Lewis eventually managed to gain access to it.[[116]](#cite_note-119)[[117]](#cite_note-120)

Meriwether Lewis and [William Clark](/wiki/William_Clark_(explorer) "William Clark (explorer)") began [their famed expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") in 1804 with a party of thirty-three people in three boats.[[118]](#cite_note-L&C2-121) Although they became the first Europeans to travel the entire length of the Missouri and reach the Pacific Ocean via the Columbia, they found no trace of the Northwest Passage. The maps made by Lewis and Clark, especially those of the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") region, provided a foundation for future explorers and emigrants. They also negotiated relations with numerous Native American tribes and wrote extensive reports on the climate, ecology and geology of the region. Many present-day names of geographic features in the upper Missouri basin originated from their expedition.[[119]](#cite_note-122)

## American frontier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: American frontier")]

### Fur trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fur trade")]

[![Painting of two figures and a cat on a boat in a placid body of water](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/George_Caleb_Bingham_001.jpg/250px-George_Caleb_Bingham_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Caleb_Bingham_001.jpg)

*Fur Traders on Missouri River*, painted by [George Caleb Bingham](/wiki/George_Caleb_Bingham "George Caleb Bingham") c. 1845

As early as the 18th century, fur trappers entered the extreme northern basin of the Missouri River in the hopes of finding populations of [beaver](/wiki/North_American_beaver "North American beaver") and [river otter](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter"), the sale of whose pelts drove the thriving [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade"). They came from many different places – some from the Canadian fur corporations at Hudson Bay, some from the Pacific Northwest (*see also*: [Maritime fur trade](/wiki/Maritime_fur_trade "Maritime fur trade")), and some from the midwestern United States. Most did not stay in the area for long, as they failed to find significant resources.[[120]](#cite_note-123)

The first glowing reports of country rich with thousands of game animals came in 1806 when Meriwether Lewis and William Clark returned from their two-year expedition. Their journals described lands amply stocked with thousands of buffalo, beaver, and river otter; and also an abundant population of [sea otters](/wiki/Sea_otter "Sea otter") on the Pacific Northwest coast. In 1807, explorer [Manuel Lisa](/wiki/Manuel_Lisa "Manuel Lisa") organized an expedition which would lead to the explosive growth of the fur trade in the upper Missouri River country. Lisa and his crew traveled up the Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers, trading manufactured items in return for furs from local Native American tribes, and established a fort at the confluence of the Yellowstone and a tributary, the [Bighorn](/wiki/Bighorn_River "Bighorn River"), in southern Montana. Although the business started small, it quickly grew into a thriving trade.[[121]](#cite_note-124)[[122]](#cite_note-125)

Lisa's men started construction of [Fort Raymond](/wiki/Fort_Raymond "Fort Raymond"), which sat on a bluff overlooking the confluence of the Yellowstone and Bighorn, in the fall of 1807. The fort would serve primarily as a trading post for bartering with the Native Americans for furs.[[123]](#cite_note-126) This method was unlike that of the Pacific Northwest fur trade, which involved trappers hired by the various fur enterprises, namely [Hudson's Bay](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"). Fort Raymond was later replaced by [Fort Lisa](/wiki/Fort_Lisa_(North_Dakota) "Fort Lisa (North Dakota)") at the confluence of the Missouri and Yellowstone in North Dakota; a second fort also called [Fort Lisa](/wiki/Fort_Lisa_(Nebraska) "Fort Lisa (Nebraska)") was built downstream on the Missouri River in Nebraska. In 1809 the [St. Louis Missouri Fur Company](/wiki/St._Louis_Missouri_Fur_Company "St. Louis Missouri Fur Company") was founded by Lisa in conjunction with William Clark and Pierre Choteau, among others.[[124]](#cite_note-127)[[125]](#cite_note-npsfur-128) In 1828, the [American Fur Company](/wiki/American_Fur_Company "American Fur Company") founded [Fort Union](/wiki/Fort_Union_Trading_Post_National_Historic_Site "Fort Union Trading Post National Historic Site") at the confluence of the Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers. Fort Union gradually became the main headquarters for the fur trade in the upper Missouri basin.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Fort_Clack_on_the_Missouri_february_1834_0048v.jpg/250px-Fort_Clack_on_the_Missouri_february_1834_0048v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Clack_on_the_Missouri_february_1834_0048v.jpg)

*[Fort Clark](/wiki/Fort_Clark_Trading_Post_State_Historic_Site "Fort Clark Trading Post State Historic Site") on the Missouri* in February 1834, painted by [Karl Bodmer](/wiki/Karl_Bodmer "Karl Bodmer")

Fur trapping activities in the early 19th century encompassed nearly all of the Rocky Mountains on both the eastern and western slopes. Trappers of the Hudson's Bay Company, St. Louis Missouri Fur Company, American Fur Company, [Rocky Mountain Fur Company](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_Fur_Company "Rocky Mountain Fur Company"), [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company") and other outfits worked thousands of streams in the Missouri watershed as well as the neighboring Columbia, Colorado, Arkansas, and Saskatchewan river systems. During this period, the trappers, also called [mountain men](/wiki/Mountain_man "Mountain man"), blazed trails through the wilderness that would later form the paths pioneers and settlers would travel by into the West. Transport of the thousands of beaver pelts required ships, providing one of the first large motives for river transport on the Missouri to start.[[127]](#cite_note-130)

As the 1830s drew to a close, the fur industry slowly began to die as silk replaced beaver fur as a desirable clothing item. By this time, also, the beaver population of streams in the Rocky Mountains had been decimated by intense hunting. Furthermore, frequent Native American attacks on trading posts made it dangerous for employees of the fur companies. In some regions, the industry continued well into the 1840s, but in others such as the Platte River valley, declines of the beaver population contributed to an earlier demise.[[128]](#cite_note-131) The fur trade finally disappeared in the Great Plains around 1850, with the primary center of industry shifting to the Mississippi Valley and central Canada. Despite the demise of the once-prosperous trade, however, its legacy led to the opening of the American West and a flood of settlers, farmers, ranchers, adventurers, hopefuls, financially bereft, and entrepreneurs took their place.[[129]](#cite_note-132)

### Settlers and pioneers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Settlers and pioneers")]

See also: [Department of the Missouri](/wiki/Department_of_the_Missouri "Department of the Missouri") and [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/George_Caleb_Bingham_-_Boatmen_on_the_Missouri_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-George_Caleb_Bingham_-_Boatmen_on_the_Missouri_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Caleb_Bingham_-_Boatmen_on_the_Missouri_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Boatmen on the Missouri* c. 1846

The river roughly defined the [American frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier") in the 19th century, particularly downstream from Kansas City, where it takes a sharp eastern turn into the heart of the state of Missouri, an area known as the [Boonslick](/wiki/Boonslick "Boonslick"). As first area settled by Europeans along the river it was largely populated by slave-owning southerners following the [Boone's Lick Road](/wiki/Boone%27s_Lick_Road "Boone's Lick Road"). The major trails for the opening of the American West all have their starting points on the river, including the [California](/wiki/California_Trail "California Trail"), [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon_Trail "Mormon Trail"), [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail"), and [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Trail "Santa Fe Trail") trails. The first westward leg of the [Pony Express](/wiki/Pony_Express "Pony Express") was a ferry across the Missouri at [St. Joseph, Missouri](/wiki/St._Joseph,_Missouri "St. Joseph, Missouri"). Similarly, most emigrants arrived at the eastern terminus of the [First transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") via a ferry ride across the Missouri between [Council Bluffs, Iowa](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"), and Omaha.[[130]](#cite_note-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134) The [Hannibal Bridge](/wiki/Hannibal_Bridge "Hannibal Bridge") became the first bridge to cross the Missouri River in 1869, and its location was a major reason why Kansas City became the largest city on the river upstream from its mouth at St. Louis.[[132]](#cite_note-135)

True to the then-ideal of [Manifest Destiny](/wiki/Manifest_Destiny "Manifest Destiny"), over 500,000 people set out from the river town of [Independence, Missouri](/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri"), to their various destinations in the American West from the 1830s to the 1860s. These people had many reasons to embark on this strenuous year-long journey – economic crisis, and later gold strikes including the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush"), for example.[[133]](#cite_note-136) For most, the route took them up the Missouri to Omaha, Nebraska, where they would [set out along the Platte River](/wiki/Great_Platte_River_Road "Great Platte River Road"), which flows from the Rocky Mountains in Wyoming and Colorado eastward through the Great Plains. An early expedition led by [Robert Stuart](/wiki/Robert_Stuart_(explorer) "Robert Stuart (explorer)") from 1812 to 1813 proved the Platte impossible to navigate by the [dugout canoes](/wiki/Dugout_canoe "Dugout canoe") they used, let alone the large sidewheelers and sternwheelers that would later ply the Missouri in increasing numbers. One explorer remarked that the Platte was "too thick to drink, too thin to plow".[[134]](#cite_note-137) Nevertheless, the Platte provided an abundant and reliable source of water for the pioneers as they headed west. Covered wagons, popularly referred to as *prairie schooners*, provided the primary means of transport until the beginning of regular boat service on the river in the 1850s.[[135]](#cite_note-138)

During the 1860s, gold strikes in Montana, Colorado, Wyoming, and northern [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") attracted another wave of hopefuls to the region. Although some freight was hauled overland, most transport to and from the gold fields was done through the Missouri and Kansas Rivers, as well as the [Snake River](/wiki/Snake_River "Snake River") in western Wyoming and the [Bear River](/wiki/Bear_River_(Great_Salt_Lake) "Bear River (Great Salt Lake)") in Utah, Idaho, and Wyoming.[[136]](#cite_note-139) It is estimated that of the passengers and freight hauled from the Midwest to Montana, over 80 percent were transported by boat, a journey that took 150 days in the upstream direction. A route more directly west into Colorado lay along the Kansas River and its tributary the Republican River as well as pair of smaller Colorado streams, [Big Sandy Creek](/wiki/Big_Sandy_Creek_(Colorado) "Big Sandy Creek (Colorado)") and the [South Platte River](/wiki/South_Platte_River "South Platte River"), to near Denver. The gold rushes precipitated the decline of the [Bozeman Trail](/wiki/Bozeman_Trail "Bozeman Trail") as a popular emigration route, as it passed through land held by often-hostile Native Americans. Safer paths were blazed to the [Great Salt Lake](/wiki/Great_Salt_Lake "Great Salt Lake") near [Corinne, Utah](/wiki/Corinne,_Utah "Corinne, Utah"), during the gold rush period, which led to the large-scale settlement of the Rocky Mountains region and eastern [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin").[[137]](#cite_note-140)

[![Painting of a fort surrounded by tepees on the bank of a river curving around a series of bluffs](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Karl_Bodmer_Travels_in_America_%2843%29.jpg/250px-Karl_Bodmer_Travels_in_America_%2843%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Bodmer_Travels_in_America_(43).jpg)

Karl Bodmer, *[Fort Pierre](/wiki/Fort_Pierre,_South_Dakota "Fort Pierre, South Dakota") and the Adjacent Prairie*, c. 1833, – the river, river bluffs and floodplain are depicted around the fort settlement

As settlers expanded their holdings into the Great Plains, they ran into land conflicts with Native American tribes. This resulted in frequent raids, massacres and armed conflicts, leading to the federal government creating multiple treaties with the Plains tribes, which generally involved establishing borders and reserving lands for the indigenous. As with many other treaties between the U.S. and Native Americans, they were soon broken, leading to huge wars. Over 1,000 battles, big and small, were fought between the U.S. military and Native Americans before the tribes were forced out of their land onto reservations.[[138]](#cite_note-141)[[139]](#cite_note-142)

Conflicts between natives and settlers over the opening of the Bozeman Trail in the Dakotas, Wyoming and Montana led to [Red Cloud's War](/wiki/Red_Cloud%27s_War "Red Cloud's War"), in which the [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") and [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne "Cheyenne") fought against the U.S. Army. The fighting resulted in a complete Native American victory.[[140]](#cite_note-143) In 1868, the [Treaty of Fort Laramie](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fort_Laramie_(1868) "Treaty of Fort Laramie (1868)") was signed, which "guaranteed" the use of the [Black Hills](/wiki/Black_Hills "Black Hills"), [Powder River Country](/wiki/Powder_River_Country "Powder River Country") and other regions surrounding the northern Missouri River to Native Americans without white intervention.[[141]](#cite_note-Laramie1868-144) The Missouri River was also a significant landmark as it divides northeastern Kansas from western Missouri; pro-slavery forces from Missouri would cross the river into Kansas and spark mayhem during [Bleeding Kansas](/wiki/Bleeding_Kansas "Bleeding Kansas"), leading to continued tension and hostility even today between [Kansas and Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_and_Missouri "Kansas and Missouri"). Another significant military engagement on the Missouri River during this period was the [1861 Battle of Boonville](/wiki/Battle_of_Boonville "Battle of Boonville"), which did not affect Native Americans but rather was a turning point in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") that allowed the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") to seize control of transport on the river, discouraging the state of Missouri from joining the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America").[[142]](#cite_note-145)

However, the peace and freedom of the Native Americans did not last for long. The [Great Sioux War of 1876](/wiki/Great_Sioux_War_of_1876 "Great Sioux War of 1876")–77 was sparked when American miners discovered gold in the Black Hills of western South Dakota and eastern Wyoming. These lands were originally set aside for Native American use by the Treaty of Fort Laramie.[[141]](#cite_note-Laramie1868-144) When the settlers intruded onto the lands, they were attacked by Native Americans. U.S. troops were sent to the area to protect the miners, and drive out the natives from the new settlements. During this bloody period, both the Native Americans and the U.S. military won victories in major battles, resulting in the loss of nearly a thousand lives. The war eventually ended in an American victory, and the Black Hills were opened to settlement. Native Americans of that region were relocated to reservations in Wyoming and southeastern Montana.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

## Dam-building era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dam-building era")]

[![Front view of a dam releasing water through its spillways](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/HolterDam1918.jpg/250px-HolterDam1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:HolterDam1918.jpg)

[Holter Dam](/wiki/Holter_Dam "Holter Dam"), a [run-of-the-river power generation dam](/wiki/Run-of-the-river_hydroelectricity "Run-of-the-river hydroelectricity") on the upper Missouri, shortly after completion in 1918

Further information: [List of dams in the Missouri River watershed](/wiki/List_of_dams_in_the_Missouri_River_watershed "List of dams in the Missouri River watershed")

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a great number of dams were built along the course of the Missouri, transforming 35 percent of the river into a chain of reservoirs.[[12]](#cite_note-Story-12) River development was stimulated by a variety of factors, first by growing demand for electricity in the rural northwestern parts of the basin, and by floods and droughts that plagued rapidly growing agricultural and urban areas along the lower Missouri River.[[144]](#cite_note-Pick-Sloan-147) Small, privately owned hydroelectric projects have existed since the 1890s, but the large flood-control and storage dams that characterize the middle reaches of the river today were not constructed until the 1950s.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28)[[144]](#cite_note-Pick-Sloan-147)

Between 1890 and 1940, five dams were built in the vicinity of [Great Falls](/wiki/Great_Falls,_Montana "Great Falls, Montana") to generate power from the [Great Falls of the Missouri](/wiki/Great_Falls_of_the_Missouri "Great Falls of the Missouri"), a chain of giant waterfalls formed by the river in its path through western Montana. [Black Eagle Dam](/wiki/Black_Eagle_Dam "Black Eagle Dam"), built in 1891 on [Black Eagle Falls](/wiki/Black_Eagle_Falls "Black Eagle Falls"), was the first dam of the Missouri.[[145]](#cite_note-148) Replaced in 1926 with a more modern structure, the dam was little more than a small weir atop Black Eagle Falls, diverting part of the Missouri's flow into the Black Eagle power plant.[[146]](#cite_note-BlackEagleDam-149) The largest of the five dams, [Ryan Dam](/wiki/Ryan_Dam "Ryan Dam"), was built in 1913. The dam lies directly above the 87-foot (27 m) [Big Falls](/wiki/Big_Falls_(Missouri_River_waterfall) "Big Falls (Missouri River waterfall)"), the largest waterfall of the Missouri.[[147]](#cite_note-RyanDam-150)

[![View of an explosion atop a dam in a flooding river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Black_Eagle_Dam_1908_DYK.jpg/250px-Black_Eagle_Dam_1908_DYK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Eagle_Dam_1908_DYK.jpg)

[Black Eagle Dam](/wiki/Black_Eagle_Dam "Black Eagle Dam") is dynamited in 1908 to save Great Falls from the flood wave caused by the failure of Hauser Dam

In the same period, several private establishments – most notably the [Montana Power Company](/wiki/Montana_Power_Company "Montana Power Company") – began to develop the Missouri River above Great Falls and below [Helena](/wiki/Helena,_Montana "Helena, Montana") for power generation. A small [run-of-the river](/wiki/Run-of-the-river_hydroelectricity "Run-of-the-river hydroelectricity") structure completed in 1898 near the present site of [Canyon Ferry Dam](/wiki/Canyon_Ferry_Dam "Canyon Ferry Dam") became the second dam built on the Missouri. This rock-filled [timber crib dam](/wiki/Dam#Timber_dams "Dam") generated seven and a half [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") of electricity for Helena and the surrounding countryside.[[148]](#cite_note-151) The nearby [steel](/wiki/Steel_dam "Steel dam") [Hauser Dam](/wiki/Hauser_Dam "Hauser Dam") was finished in 1907, but failed in 1908 because of structural deficiencies, causing catastrophic flooding all the way downstream past [Craig](/wiki/Craig,_Montana "Craig, Montana"). At Great Falls, a section of the Black Eagle Dam was dynamited to save nearby factories from inundation.[[149]](#cite_note-152) Hauser was rebuilt in 1910 as a concrete gravity structure, and stands to this day.[[150]](#cite_note-Hauser-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154)

[Holter Dam](/wiki/Holter_Dam "Holter Dam"), about 45 miles (72 km) downstream of Helena, was the third hydroelectric dam built on this stretch of the Missouri River.[[152]](#cite_note-HolterDam-155) When completed in 1918 by the Montana Power Company and the United Missouri River Power Company, its reservoir flooded the [Gates of the Mountains](/wiki/Gates_of_the_Mountains_Wilderness "Gates of the Mountains Wilderness"), a limestone canyon which Meriwether Lewis described as "the most remarkable clifts that we have yet seen ... the tow[er]ing and projecting rocks in many places seem ready to tumble on us."[[153]](#cite_note-156) In 1949, the [U.S. Bureau of Reclamation](/wiki/U.S._Bureau_of_Reclamation "U.S. Bureau of Reclamation") (USBR) began construction on the modern Canyon Ferry Dam to provide flood control to the Great Falls area. By 1954, the rising waters of [Canyon Ferry Lake](/wiki/Canyon_Ferry_Lake "Canyon Ferry Lake") submerged the old 1898 dam, whose powerhouse still stands underwater about 1+1⁄2 miles (2.4 km) upstream of the present-day dam.[[154]](#cite_note-CanyonFerryDam-157)

> [ The Missouri's temperament was as ] "uncertain as the actions of a jury or the state of a woman's mind".
>
> — *Sioux City Register*, March 28, 1868[[155]](#cite_note-RNA438-158)

The Missouri basin suffered a series of catastrophic floods around the turn of the 20th century, most notably in [1844](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1844 "Great Flood of 1844"), [1881](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1881 "Great Flood of 1881"), and [1926–1927](/wiki/Great_Mississippi_Flood_of_1927 "Great Mississippi Flood of 1927").[[156]](#cite_note-flooding-159) In 1940, as part of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression")-era [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"), the [U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers") (USACE) completed Fort Peck Dam in Montana. Construction of this massive public works project provided jobs for more than 50,000 laborers during the Depression and was a major step in providing flood control to the lower half of the Missouri River.[[157]](#cite_note-160) However, Fort Peck only controls runoff from 11 percent of the Missouri River watershed, and had little effect on a severe snowmelt flood that struck the lower basin three years later. This event was particularly destructive as it submerged manufacturing plants in Omaha and Kansas City, greatly delaying shipments of military supplies in World War II.[[156]](#cite_note-flooding-159)[[158]](#cite_note-161)

[![Map showing major dams and reservoirs in the Missouri River basin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Pick-Sloan_Plan.png/500px-Pick-Sloan_Plan.png)](/wiki/File:Pick-Sloan_Plan.png)

Map showing major features of the [Pick–Sloan Plan](/wiki/Pick%E2%80%93Sloan_Plan "Pick–Sloan Plan"); other dams and their reservoirs are denoted by triangles

Flooding damages on the Mississippi–Missouri river system were one of the primary reasons for which [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") passed the [Flood Control Act of 1944](/wiki/Flood_Control_Act_of_1944 "Flood Control Act of 1944"), opening the way for the [USACE](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers") to develop the Missouri on a massive scale.[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163) The 1944 act authorized the [Pick–Sloan Missouri Basin Program](/wiki/Pick%E2%80%93Sloan_Missouri_Basin_Program "Pick–Sloan Missouri Basin Program") (Pick–Sloan Plan), which was a composite of two widely varying proposals. The Pick plan, with an emphasis on flood control and hydroelectric power, called for the construction of large storage dams along the main stem of the Missouri. The Sloan plan, which stressed the development of local irrigation, included provisions for roughly 85 smaller dams on tributaries.[[144]](#cite_note-Pick-Sloan-147)[[161]](#cite_note-Otstot-164)

In the early stages of Pick–Sloan development, tentative plans were made to build a low dam on the Missouri at Riverdale, North Dakota, and 27 smaller dams on the Yellowstone River and its tributaries.[[162]](#cite_note-165) This was met with controversy from inhabitants of the Yellowstone basin, and eventually the [USBR](/w/index.php?title=Bureau_of_ReclamationB&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bureau of ReclamationB (page does not exist)") proposed a solution: to greatly increase the size of the proposed dam at Riverdale – today's Garrison Dam, thus replacing the storage that would have been provided by the Yellowstone dams. Because of this decision, the Yellowstone is now the longest free-flowing river in the contiguous United States.[[163]](#cite_note-166) In the 1950s, construction commenced on the five mainstem dams – Garrison, Oahe, [Big Bend](/wiki/Big_Bend_Dam "Big Bend Dam"), [Fort Randall](/wiki/Fort_Randall_Dam "Fort Randall Dam"), and [Gavins Point](/wiki/Gavins_Point_Dam "Gavins Point Dam") – proposed under the Pick-Sloan Plan.[[144]](#cite_note-Pick-Sloan-147) Along with Fort Peck, which was integrated as a unit of the Pick-Sloan Plan in the 1940s, these dams now form the Missouri River Mainstem System.[[164]](#cite_note-Mainstem-167)

The flooding of lands along the Missouri River heavily impacted Native American groups whose reservations included fertile bottomlands and floodplains, especially in the arid Dakotas where it was some of the only good farmland they had. These consequences were pronounced in North Dakota's [Fort Berthold Indian Reservation](/wiki/Fort_Berthold_Indian_Reservation "Fort Berthold Indian Reservation"), where 150,000 acres (61,000 ha) of land was taken by the construction of Garrison Dam. The Mandan, Hidatsa and Arikara / Sanish tribes sued the federal government on the basis of the [1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fort_Laramie_(1851) "Treaty of Fort Laramie (1851)") which provided that reservation land could not be taken without the consent of both the tribes and Congress. After a lengthy legal battle the tribes were coerced in 1947 to accept a $5.1 million ($55 million today) settlement for the land, just $33 per acre. In 1949 this was increased to $12.6 million. The tribes were even denied the right to use the reservoir shore "for grazing, hunting, fishing, and other purposes".[[165]](#cite_note-168)[[166]](#cite_note-169)

[![Aerial view of a large earthfill dam with its reservoir on the left and background, surrounded by brownish hills](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Fort_Peck_Dam_%28Fort_Peck_Montana%29_1986_01.jpg/330px-Fort_Peck_Dam_%28Fort_Peck_Montana%29_1986_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Peck_Dam_(Fort_Peck_Montana)_1986_01.jpg)

[Fort Peck Dam](/wiki/Fort_Peck_Dam "Fort Peck Dam"), the uppermost dam of the Missouri River Mainstem System

The six dams of the Mainstem System, chiefly Fort Peck, Garrison, and Oahe, are among [the largest dams in the world by volume](/wiki/List_of_largest_dams_in_the_world "List of largest dams in the world"); their sprawling reservoirs also rank among [the biggest of the nation](/wiki/List_of_largest_reservoirs_in_the_United_States "List of largest reservoirs in the United States").[[167]](#cite_note-170) Holding up to 74.1 million acre-feet (91.4 km3) in total, the six reservoirs can store more than three years' worth of the river's flow as measured below Gavins Point, the lowermost dam.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) This capacity makes it the largest reservoir system in the United States and one of the largest in North America.[[168]](#cite_note-171) In addition to storing irrigation water, the system also includes an annual flood-control reservation of 16.3 million acre-feet (20.1 km3).[[164]](#cite_note-Mainstem-167) Mainstem power plants generate about 9.3 billion [KWh](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour "Kilowatt hour") annually – equal to a constant output of almost 1,100 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt").[[169]](#cite_note-172) Along with nearly 100 smaller dams on tributaries, namely the [Bighorn](/wiki/Bighorn_River "Bighorn River"), Platte, Kansas, and Osage Rivers, the system provides irrigation water to nearly 7,500 mi2 (19,000 km2) of land.[[144]](#cite_note-Pick-Sloan-147)[[170]](#cite_note-173)
