## Navigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Navigation")]

> [ Missouri River shipping ] "never achieved its expectations. Even under the very best of circumstances, it was never a huge industry".
>
> — Richard Opper, former executive director  
> Missouri River Basin Association[[182]](#cite_note-decline-186)

[![Painting of a steamboat stranded on a sandbar in the middle of a swift-flowing river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Yellowstone_%28steamboat%29_aground.jpg/250px-Yellowstone_%28steamboat%29_aground.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yellowstone_(steamboat)_aground.jpg)

Painting of the steamboat *Yellowstone*, one of the earliest commercial vessels to run on the river, circa 1833. The dangerous currents in the river caused the ship to run aground on a [sandbar](/wiki/Sandbar "Sandbar") in this illustration.

Boat travel on the Missouri began with the wood-framed canoes and [bull boats](/wiki/Bull_boat "Bull boat") that Native Americans used for thousands of years before the colonization of the Great Plains introduced larger craft to the river.[[183]](#cite_note-boats-187) The first steamboat on the Missouri was the *Independence*, which started running between St. Louis and [Keytesville, Missouri](/wiki/Keytesville,_Missouri "Keytesville, Missouri"), around 1819.[[184]](#cite_note-D101-188) By the 1830s, large mail and freight-carrying vessels were running regularly between Kansas City and St. Louis, and many traveled even farther upstream. A handful, such as the *[Western Engineer](/wiki/Western_Engineer "Western Engineer")* and the *[Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_(steamboat) "Yellowstone (steamboat)")*, could make it up the river as far as eastern Montana.[[183]](#cite_note-boats-187)[[185]](#cite_note-189)

During the early 19th century, at the height of the fur trade, steamboats and [keelboats](/wiki/Keelboat "Keelboat") travelled nearly the whole length of the Missouri from Montana's rugged Missouri Breaks to the mouth, carrying beaver and buffalo furs to and from the areas the trappers frequented.[[186]](#cite_note-Boonslick-190) This resulted in the development of the [Missouri River mackinaw](/wiki/Mackinaw_boat#Development "Mackinaw boat"), which specialized in carrying furs. Since these boats could only travel downriver, they were dismantled and sold for lumber upon their arrival at St. Louis.[[183]](#cite_note-boats-187)

Water transport increased through the 1850s with multiple craft ferrying pioneers, emigrants and miners; many of these runs were from St. Louis or Independence to near Omaha. There, most of these people would set out overland along the large but shallow and unnavigable Platte River, which pioneers described as "a mile wide and an inch deep" and "the most magnificent and useless of rivers".[[187]](#cite_note-191) Steamboat navigation peaked in 1858 with over 130 boats operating full-time on the Missouri, with many more smaller vessels.[[188]](#cite_note-Dyer2-192) Many of the earlier vessels were built on the Ohio River before being transferred to the Missouri. Side-wheeler steamboats were preferred over the larger sternwheelers used on the Mississippi and Ohio because of their greater maneuverability.[[186]](#cite_note-Boonslick-190)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Far_West_%28steamship%29.jpg/250px-Far_West_%28steamship%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Far_West_(steamship).jpg)

The *[Far West](/wiki/Far_West_(River_Steamboat) "Far West (River Steamboat)")* is typical of the shallow-draft steamboats used to navigate the Missouri River. Famed captain and pilot [Grant Marsh](/wiki/Grant_Marsh "Grant Marsh") set several speed records, including one taking wounded soldiers from the surviving segments of Custer's [Black Hills Expedition](/wiki/Black_Hills_Expedition "Black Hills Expedition") to get medical care.[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[190]](#cite_note-194)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Missouri_river_at_hwy_364.jpg/250px-Missouri_river_at_hwy_364.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouri_river_at_hwy_364.jpg)

A barge travels North on the Missouri River at Highway 364 in [Saint Charles, Missouri](/wiki/Saint_Charles,_Missouri "Saint Charles, Missouri")

The industry's success, however, did not guarantee safety. In the early decades before man controlled the river's flow, its sketchy rises and falls and its massive amounts of sediment, which prevented a clear view of the bottom, wrecked some 300 vessels. Because of the dangers of navigating the Missouri River, the average ship's lifespan was only about four years.[[188]](#cite_note-Dyer2-192) The development of the Transcontinental and [Northern Pacific](/wiki/Northern_Pacific_Railroad "Northern Pacific Railroad") Railroads marked the beginning of the end of steamboat commerce on the Missouri. Outcompeted by trains, the number of boats slowly dwindled, until there was almost nothing left by the 1890s. Transport of agricultural and mining products by barge, however, saw a revival in the early twentieth century.[[191]](#cite_note-195)[[192]](#cite_note-196)

### Passage to Sioux City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Passage to Sioux City")]

Since the beginning of the 20th century, the Missouri River has been extensively engineered for water transport purposes, and about 32 percent of the river now flows through artificially straightened channels.[[12]](#cite_note-Story-12) In 1912, the USACE was authorized to maintain the Missouri to a depth of six feet (1.8 meters) from the [Port of Kansas City](/wiki/Port_of_Kansas_City "Port of Kansas City") to the mouth, a distance of 368 miles (592 km).[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18) This was accomplished by constructing levees and [wing dams](/wiki/Wing_dam "Wing dam") to direct the river's flow into a straight, narrow channel and prevent sedimentation. In 1925, the USACE began a project to widen the river's navigation channel to 200 feet (61 m); two years later, they began dredging a deep-water channel from Kansas City to Sioux City. These modifications have reduced the river's length from some 2,540 miles (4,090 km) in the late 19th century to 2,341 miles (3,767 km) in the present day.[[6]](#cite_note-modifications-6)[[193]](#cite_note-197)

[![Side view of a dam surrounded by green hills under a clear sky](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Gavins_Point_Dam.jpg/250px-Gavins_Point_Dam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gavins_Point_Dam.jpg)

[Gavins Point Dam](/wiki/Gavins_Point_Dam "Gavins Point Dam") at [Yankton, South Dakota](/wiki/Yankton,_South_Dakota "Yankton, South Dakota"), is the uppermost obstacle to navigation from the mouth on the Missouri today.

Construction of dams on the Missouri under the Pick-Sloan Plan in the mid-twentieth century was the final step in aiding navigation. The large reservoirs of the Mainstem System help provide a dependable flow to maintain the navigation channel year-round, and are capable of halting most of the river's annual freshets.[[194]](#cite_note-198) However, high and low water cycles of the Missouri – notably the protracted early-21st-century drought in the Missouri River basin[[195]](#cite_note-199) and historic floods in 1993[[196]](#cite_note-200) and 2011[[197]](#cite_note-2011flood1-201) – are difficult for even the massive Mainstem System reservoirs to control.[[197]](#cite_note-2011flood1-201)

In 1945, the USACE began the Missouri River Bank Stabilization and Navigation Project, which would permanently increase the river's navigation channel to a width of 300 feet (91 m) and a depth of nine feet (2.7 meters). During work that continues to this day, the 735-mile (1,183 km) navigation channel from Sioux City to St. Louis has been controlled by building rock dikes to direct the river's flow and scour out sediments, sealing and cutting off meanders and side channels, and dredging the riverbed.[[198]](#cite_note-202) However, the Missouri has often resisted the efforts of the USACE to control its depth. In 2006, the [U.S. Coast Guard](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard "U.S. Coast Guard") stated that commercial barge tows ran aground in the Missouri River because the navigation channel had been severely silted.[[199]](#cite_note-203) The USACE was blamed for failing to maintain the channel to the minimum depth.[[200]](#cite_note-204)

[![Aerial view of a brownish river winding through an agricultural valley](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Missouririver1.jpg/250px-Missouririver1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouririver1.jpg)

The Missouri River near [New Haven, Missouri](/wiki/New_Haven,_Missouri "New Haven, Missouri"), looking upstream – note the [riprap](/wiki/Riprap "Riprap") [wing dam](/wiki/Wing_dam "Wing dam") protruding into the river from the left to direct its flow into a narrower channel

[![View of two rivers meeting in the middle of an industrial area](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Missouri-Floyd_Rivers_Sioux_City_Iowa.jpg/250px-Missouri-Floyd_Rivers_Sioux_City_Iowa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouri-Floyd_Rivers_Sioux_City_Iowa.jpg)

The Missouri River at the confluence with the [Floyd River](/wiki/Floyd_River "Floyd River") in [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), near the upper most navigable reach of the river today

In 1929, the [Missouri River Navigation Commission](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River_Navigation_Commission&action=edit&redlink=1 "Missouri River Navigation Commission (page does not exist)") estimated the amount of goods shipped on the river annually at 15 million tons (13.6 million metric tons), providing widespread consensus for the creation of a navigation channel. However, shipping traffic has since been far lower than expected – shipments of commodities including produce, manufactured items, lumber, and oil averaged only 683,000 tons (616,000 t) per year from 1994 to 2006.[[201]](#cite_note-GAO-205)

By tonnage of transported material, Missouri is by far the largest user of the river accounting for 83 percent of river traffic, while Kansas has 12 percent, Nebraska three percent and Iowa two percent. Almost all of the barge traffic on the Missouri River ships sand and gravel dredged from the lower 500 miles (800 km) of the river; the remaining portion of the shipping channel now sees little to no use by commercial vessels.[[201]](#cite_note-GAO-205)

For navigation purposes, the Missouri River is divided into two main sections. The Upper Missouri River is north of [Gavins Point Dam](/wiki/Gavins_Point_Dam "Gavins Point Dam"), the last hydroelectric dam of [fifteen](/wiki/List_of_dams_in_the_Missouri_River_watershed "List of dams in the Missouri River watershed") on the river, just upstream from [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa").[[202]](#cite_note-206) The Lower Missouri River is the 840 miles (1,350 km) of river below Gavins Point until it meets the Mississippi just above [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"). The Lower Missouri River has no [hydroelectric dams](/wiki/Hydroelectric_dam "Hydroelectric dam") or [locks](/wiki/Lock_(water_transport) "Lock (water transport)") but it has a plethora of [wing dams](/wiki/Wing_dam "Wing dam") that enable [barge](/wiki/Barge "Barge") traffic by directing the flow of the river into a 200-foot-wide (61 m), 12-foot-deep (3.7 m) channel. These wing dams have been put in place by and are maintained by the [U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers"), and there are no plans to construct any locks to replace these wing dams on the Missouri River.

### Traffic decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Traffic decline")]

Tonnage of goods shipped by barges on the Missouri River has seen a serious decline from the 1960s to the present. In the 1960s, the USACE predicted an increase to 12 million short tons (11 Mt) per year by 2000, but instead the opposite has happened. The amount of goods plunged from 3.3 million short tons (3.0 Mt) in 1977 to just 1.3 million short tons (1.2 Mt) in 2000.[[203]](#cite_note-207) One of the largest drops has been in agricultural products, especially wheat. Part of the reason is that irrigated land along the Missouri has only been developed to a fraction of its potential.[[204]](#cite_note-Prairiefire-208) In 2006, barges on the Missouri hauled only 200,000 short tons (180,000 t) of products which is equal to the *daily* freight traffic on the Mississippi.[[204]](#cite_note-Prairiefire-208)

Drought conditions in the early 21st century and competition from other modes of transport – mainly railroads – are the primary reason for decreasing river traffic on the Missouri. The USACE's failure to consistently maintain the navigation channel has also hampered the industry. Efforts are being made to revive the shipping industry on the Missouri River, because of the efficiency and cheapness of river transport to haul agricultural products, and the overcrowding of alternative transportation routes. Solutions such as expanding the navigation channel and releasing more water from reservoirs during the peak of the navigation season are under consideration.[[205]](#cite_note-209)
Drought conditions lifted in 2010, in which about 334,000 short tons (303,000 t) were barged on the Missouri, representing the first significant increase in shipments since 2000. However, flooding in 2011 closed record stretches of the river to boat traffic – "wash[ing] away hopes for a bounce-back year".[[206]](#cite_note-210)

There are no lock and dams on the lower Missouri River, but there are plenty of [wing dams](/wiki/Wing_dam "Wing dam") that [jettie](/wiki/Jetty "Jetty") out into the river and make it harder for barges to navigate. In contrast, the upper Mississippi has [29 locks and dams](/wiki/List_of_locks_and_dams_of_the_Upper_Mississippi_River "List of locks and dams of the Upper Mississippi River") and averaged 61.3 million tons of cargo annually from 2008 to 2011,[[207]](#cite_note-211) and its locks are closed in the winter.[[208]](#cite_note-212)[[209]](#cite_note-213)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ecology")]

### Natural history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Natural history")]

[![Map showing the three freshwater ecoregions of the Missouri River basin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Missouririverecoregions.jpg/330px-Missouririverecoregions.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missouririverecoregions.jpg)

Freshwater [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") of the Missouri basin

Historically, the thousands of square miles that comprised the floodplain of the Missouri River supported a wide range of plant and animal species. Biodiversity generally increased proceeding downstream from the cold, subalpine headwaters in Montana to the temperate, moist climate of Missouri. Today, the river's [riparian zone](/wiki/Riparian_zone "Riparian zone") consists primarily of [cottonwoods](/wiki/Hibiscus_tiliaceus "Hibiscus tiliaceus"), [willows](/wiki/Willow "Willow") and [sycamores](/wiki/Platanus "Platanus"), with several other types of trees such as [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple") and [ash](/wiki/Fraxinus "Fraxinus").[[210]](#cite_note-RNA436-214) Average tree height generally increases farther from the riverbanks for a limited distance, as land next to the river is vulnerable to soil erosion during floods. Because of its large sediment concentrations, the Missouri does not support many aquatic invertebrates.[[210]](#cite_note-RNA436-214) However, the basin supports about 300 species of birds[[210]](#cite_note-RNA436-214) and 150 species of fish,[[211]](#cite_note-215) some of which are [endangered](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species") such as the [pallid sturgeon](/wiki/Pallid_sturgeon "Pallid sturgeon"). The Missouri's aquatic and riparian habitats also support several species of mammals, such as [minks](/wiki/Mink "Mink"), river otters, beavers, [muskrats](/wiki/Muskrat "Muskrat"), and [raccoons](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon").[[155]](#cite_note-RNA438-158)

The [World Wide Fund For Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_For_Nature "World Wide Fund For Nature") divides the Missouri River watershed into three freshwater [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"): the Upper Missouri, Lower Missouri and Central Prairie. The Upper Missouri, roughly encompassing the area within Montana, Wyoming, southern Alberta and Saskatchewan, and North Dakota, comprises mainly semiarid [shrub-steppe](/wiki/Shrub-steppe "Shrub-steppe") grasslands with sparse biodiversity because of [Ice Age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") [glaciations](/wiki/Glaciation "Glaciation"). There are no known [endemic species](/wiki/Endemic_species "Endemic species") within the region. Except for the headwaters in the Rockies, there is little precipitation in this part of the watershed.[[212]](#cite_note-216) The Middle Missouri ecoregion, extending through Colorado, southwestern Minnesota, northern Kansas, Nebraska, and parts of Wyoming and Iowa, has greater rainfall and is characterized by temperate forests and grasslands. Plant life is more diverse in the Middle Missouri, which is also home to about twice as many animal species.[[213]](#cite_note-217) Finally, the Central Prairie ecoregion is situated on the lower part of the Missouri, encompassing all or parts of Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma and Arkansas. Despite large seasonal temperature fluctuations, this region has the greatest diversity of plants and animals of the three. Thirteen species of [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish") are endemic to the lower Missouri.[[214]](#cite_note-218)

### Human impacts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Human impacts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Mo_River_Courthouse%2C_GF_Montana_2.jpg/250px-Mo_River_Courthouse%2C_GF_Montana_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mo_River_Courthouse,_GF_Montana_2.jpg)

Missouri River as it flows through [Great Falls, Montana](/wiki/Great_Falls,_Montana "Great Falls, Montana")

Since river commerce and industrial development began in the 1800s, human activity has severely polluted the Missouri and degraded its water quality. Most of the river's floodplain habitat is long gone, replaced by irrigated agricultural land. Development of the floodplain has led to increasing numbers of people and infrastructure within areas at high risk of inundation. Levees have been constructed along more than a third of the river to keep floodwater within the channel, but with the consequences of faster stream velocity and a resulting increase of peak flows in downstream areas. [Fertilizer](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") runoff, which causes elevated levels of nitrogen and other nutrients, is a major problem along the Missouri River, especially in Iowa and Missouri. This form of pollution also affects the upper Mississippi, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") and Ohio Rivers. Low oxygen levels in rivers and the vast [Gulf of Mexico dead zone](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico_dead_zone "Gulf of Mexico dead zone") at the end of the Mississippi Delta are both results of high nutrient concentrations in the Missouri and other tributaries of the Mississippi.[[215]](#cite_note-219)

[![View of the confluence of two rivers in an agricultural area](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Nishnabotna_River_aerial.jpg/250px-Nishnabotna_River_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nishnabotna_River_aerial.jpg)

Agricultural fields dominate most of the former [floodplain](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain"), including this area around the Missouri's confluence with the [Nishnabotna River](/wiki/Nishnabotna_River "Nishnabotna River") in western Missouri.

Channelization of the lower Missouri waters has made the river narrower, deeper and less accessible to riparian flora and fauna. Many dams and bank stabilization projects have been built to help convert 300,000 acres (1,200 km2) of Missouri River floodplain to agricultural land. Channel control has reduced the volume of sediment transported downstream by the river and eliminated critical habitat for fish, birds and amphibians.[[216]](#cite_note-riparianloss-220) By the early 21st century, declines in populations of native species prompted the [U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/U.S._Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service") to issue a biological opinion recommending restoration of river habitats for federally endangered bird and fish species.[[217]](#cite_note-221)

The USACE began work on ecosystem restoration projects along the lower Missouri River in the early 21st century. Because of the low use of the shipping channel in the lower Missouri maintained by the USACE, it is now considered feasible to remove some of the levees, dikes, and wing dams that constrict the river's flow, thus allowing it to naturally restore its banks.[[216]](#cite_note-riparianloss-220) By 2001, there were 87,000 acres (350 km2) of riverside floodplain undergoing active restoration.[[218]](#cite_note-222)

Restoration projects have re-mobilized some of the sediments that had been trapped behind bank stabilization structures, prompting concerns of exacerbated nutrient and sediment pollution locally and downstream in the northern Gulf of Mexico. A 2010 [National Research Council](/wiki/United_States_National_Research_Council "United States National Research Council") report assessed the roles of sediment in the Missouri River, evaluating current habitat restoration strategies and alternative ways to manage sediment.[[219]](#cite_note-223) The report found that a better understanding of sediment processes in the Missouri River, including the creation of a "sediment budget" – an accounting of sediment transport, erosion, and deposition volumes for the length of the Missouri River – would provide a foundation for projects to improve water quality standards and protect endangered species.[[220]](#cite_note-224)

### National Wild and Scenic River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: National Wild and Scenic River")]

Several sections of the Missouri River were added to the [National Wild and Scenic Rivers System](/wiki/National_Wild_and_Scenic_Rivers_System "National Wild and Scenic Rivers System") from [Fort Benton](/wiki/Fort_Benton,_Montana "Fort Benton, Montana") to [Robinson Bridge](/wiki/Fred_Robinson_Bridge "Fred Robinson Bridge"), [Gavins Point Dam](/wiki/Gavins_Point_Dam "Gavins Point Dam") to [Ponca State Park](/wiki/Ponca_State_Park "Ponca State Park") and [Fort Randall Dam](/wiki/Fort_Randall_Dam "Fort Randall Dam") to [Lewis and Clark Lake](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Lake "Lewis and Clark Lake"). A total of 247 miles (398 km) of the river were designated including 64 miles (103 km) of wild river and 26 miles (42 km) of scenic river in Montana. 157 miles (253 km) of the river is listed as recreational under the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System.

## Tourism and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Tourism and recreation")]

[![View of a river winding past a sandbar with people on the shore](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/MNRR_At_the_North_Alabama_wreck_site_20070807171612.jpg/250px-MNRR_At_the_North_Alabama_wreck_site_20070807171612.jpg)](/wiki/File:MNRR_At_the_North_Alabama_wreck_site_20070807171612.jpg)

Part of the [Missouri National Recreational River](/wiki/Missouri_National_Recreational_River "Missouri National Recreational River"), a 98-mile (158 km) preserved stretch of the Missouri on the border of South Dakota and Nebraska

With over 1,500 mi2 (3,900 km2) of open water, the six reservoirs of the Missouri River Mainstem System provide some of the main recreational areas within the basin. Visitation has increased from 10 million visitor-hours in the mid-1960s to over 60 million visitor-hours in 1990.[[204]](#cite_note-Prairiefire-208) Development of visitor facilities was spurred by the Federal Water Project Recreation Act of 1965, which required the USACE to build and maintain boat ramps, campgrounds and other public facilities along major reservoirs.[[25]](#cite_note-MainstemSystem-28) Recreational use of Missouri River reservoirs is estimated to contribute $85–100 million to the regional economy each year.[[221]](#cite_note-TodaysMissouri-225)

The [Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_National_Historic_Trail "Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail"), some 3,700 miles (6,000 km) long, follows nearly the entire Missouri River from its mouth to its source, retracing the route of the Lewis and Clark Expedition. Extending from [Wood River, Illinois](/wiki/Wood_River,_Illinois "Wood River, Illinois"), in the east, to [Astoria, Oregon](/wiki/Astoria,_Oregon "Astoria, Oregon"), in the west, it also follows portions of the Mississippi and Columbia Rivers. The trail, which spans through eleven U.S. states, is maintained by various federal and state government agencies; it passes through some 100 historic sites, notably archaeological locations including the [Knife River Indian Villages National Historic Site](/wiki/Knife_River_Indian_Villages_National_Historic_Site "Knife River Indian Villages National Historic Site").[[222]](#cite_note-226)[[223]](#cite_note-227)

Parts of the river itself are designated for recreational or preservational use. The [Missouri National Recreational River](/wiki/Missouri_National_Recreational_River "Missouri National Recreational River") consists of portions of the Missouri downstream from Fort Randall and Gavins Point Dams that total 98 miles (158 km).[[224]](#cite_note-228)[[225]](#cite_note-229) These reaches exhibit islands, meanders, sandbars, underwater rocks, [riffles](/wiki/Riffle-pool_sequence "Riffle-pool sequence"), [snags](/wiki/Snag_(ecology) "Snag (ecology)"), and other once-common features of the lower river that have now disappeared under reservoirs or have been destroyed by channeling. About forty-five steamboat wrecks are scattered along these reaches of the river.[[226]](#cite_note-230)[[227]](#cite_note-231)

Downstream from Great Falls, Montana, about 149 miles (240 km) of the river course through a rugged series of canyons and badlands known as the Missouri Breaks. This part of the river, designated a U.S. [National Wild and Scenic River](/wiki/National_Wild_and_Scenic_River "National Wild and Scenic River") in 1976, flows within the [Upper Missouri Breaks National Monument](/wiki/Upper_Missouri_Breaks_National_Monument "Upper Missouri Breaks National Monument"), a 375,000-acre (1,520 km2) preserve comprising steep cliffs, deep gorges, arid plains, badlands, archaeological sites, and whitewater rapids on the Missouri itself. The preserve includes a wide variety of plant and animal life; recreational activities include boating, rafting, hiking and wildlife observation.[[228]](#cite_note-232)[[229]](#cite_note-233)

In north-central Montana, some 1,100,000 acres (4,500 km2) along over 125 miles (201 km) of the Missouri River, centering on [Fort Peck Lake](/wiki/Fort_Peck_Lake "Fort Peck Lake"), comprise the [Charles M. Russell National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Charles_M._Russell_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Charles M. Russell National Wildlife Refuge").[[230]](#cite_note-234) The wildlife refuge consists of a native northern Great Plains ecosystem that has not been heavily affected by human development, except for the construction of Fort Peck Dam. Although there are few designated trails, the whole preserve is open to hiking and camping.[[231]](#cite_note-235)

Many U.S. national parks, such as [Glacier National Park](/wiki/Glacier_National_Park_(U.S.) "Glacier National Park (U.S.)"), [Rocky Mountain National Park](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_National_Park "Rocky Mountain National Park"), [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") and [Badlands National Park](/wiki/Badlands_National_Park "Badlands National Park") are, at least partially, in the watershed. Parts of other rivers in the basin are set aside for preservation and recreational use – notably the [Niobrara National Scenic River](/wiki/Niobrara_National_Scenic_River "Niobrara National Scenic River"), which is a 76-mile (122 km) protected stretch of the Niobrara River, one of the Missouri's longest tributaries.[[232]](#cite_note-236) The Missouri flows through or past many [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), which include [Three Forks of the Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_Headwaters_State_Park "Missouri Headwaters State Park"),[[233]](#cite_note-237) [Fort Benton, Montana](/wiki/Fort_Benton,_Montana "Fort Benton, Montana"),[[234]](#cite_note-238) [Big Hidatsa Village Site](/wiki/Big_Hidatsa_Village_Site "Big Hidatsa Village Site"),[[235]](#cite_note-239) [Fort Atkinson, Nebraska](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson_(Nebraska) "Fort Atkinson (Nebraska)")[[236]](#cite_note-240) and [Arrow Rock Historic District](/wiki/Arrow_Rock_Historic_District "Arrow Rock Historic District").[[237]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Cow_Creek_flows_into_Missouri%2C_near_Cow_Island_in_Missouri_Breaks%2C_Blaine_County%2C_Montana.JPG/960px-Cow_Creek_flows_into_Missouri%2C_near_Cow_Island_in_Missouri_Breaks%2C_Blaine_County%2C_Montana.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cow_Creek_flows_into_Missouri,_near_Cow_Island_in_Missouri_Breaks,_Blaine_County,_Montana.JPG "The Missouri River in Upper Missouri Breaks National Monument, Montana, at the confluence with Cow Creek")

The Missouri River in [Upper Missouri Breaks National Monument](/wiki/Upper_Missouri_Breaks_National_Monument "Upper Missouri Breaks National Monument"), Montana, at the confluence with [Cow Creek](/wiki/Cow_Creek_(Montana) "Cow Creek (Montana)")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missouri_River&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** 
   The Missouri's flow at [Culbertson, Montana](/wiki/Culbertson,_Montana "Culbertson, Montana"), 25 mi (40 km) above the confluence of the two rivers, is about 9,820 cu ft/s (278 m3/s)[[19]](#cite_note-19) and the Yellowstone's discharge at [Sidney, Montana](/wiki/Sidney,_Montana "Sidney, Montana"), roughly the same distance upstream along that river, is about 12,370 cu ft/s (350 m3/s).[[20]](#cite_note-20)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** 
   The Mississippi River flows for approximately 1,172 miles (1,886 km) above St. Louis,[[18]](#cite_note-ACMEmapper-18) which is just over half of the Missouri's length.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** 
   The Mississippi drains an area of 172,200 mi2 (446,000 km2) above the confluence with the Missouri River.[[14]](#cite_note-USGS-14)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-175)** 
   "Long Pool" is the name used by area residents to refer to the smooth, almost lake-like 55 mi (89 km) stretch of the Missouri between the Black Eagle Dam and the town of [Cascade](/wiki/Cascade,_Montana "Cascade, Montana"). Only about 2 mi (3.2 km) of the so-called Long Pool are actually part of the impoundment behind the dam.
